nativeRange.habitat: Soft, mineral-poor, slightly acidic freshwater streams of Guangdong Province (Yingde and Conghua counties) and Hong Kong. The selectively-bred Taiwan Bee color morphs are aquaculture-only and have no wild population; the line was developed in Taiwan from C. mariae / C. cantonensis stock starting in the late 1990s.

decorPreferences: active buffering substrate (Brightwell Rio Escuro, ADA Amazonia, or equivalent acidic soil)
Indian almond leaves and other botanicals
sponge filter with shrimp-safe intake guard
live plants tolerant of soft acidic water

diet.notes: Grazes primarily on biofilm and soft algae. Has been documented accepting specialty Caridina-formulated shrimp foods, blanched vegetables (spinach, nettle, mulberry leaves, moringa), and decaying botanicals. Mineral supplementation (cuttlebone, mineral stones) is commonly recommended to support molting. Overfeeding is widely cited as a leading cause of die-offs; small portions every other day are generally advised.

goodWith: other Caridina shrimp on the same parameter line (Crystal complex)
very small peaceful nano fish (chili rasboras, ember tetras) in larger setups
avoidWith: Neocaridina shrimp (incompatible parameters; can also interbreed-confuse genetics in some hybrid lines)
cichlids, gouramis, bettas, large barbs and tetras

summary: A high-grade selectively-bred dwarf shrimp complex (King Kong, Panda, Blue Bolt, Wine Red, Shadow Mosura, etc.) requiring soft, acidic, low-TDS water. Generally considered an advanced shrimp due to tight parameter tolerances.
careNotes:
Foundation entry covering the Taiwan Bee shrimp complex — a group of selectively-bred high-grade color morphs (King Kong, Panda, Blue Bolt, Wine Red / Red Wine, Black King Kong, Shadow Mosura, Shadow Panda, Extreme Black King Kong, Pinto, Red Ruby) developed in Taiwan from C. mariae / C. cantonensis stock. All these morphs share identical husbandry requirements and will interbreed.

Taxonomy is fluid: some sources still classify the entire Taiwan Bee complex under C. cantonensis (the Crystal Bee parent species), while others — including Klotz & von Rintelen's 2014 description of C. mariae — recognise it as a sister species. This entry uses C. mariae as the canonical scientific name per the more recent literature, but reviewers will see C. cantonensis in many trade and hobby sources.

Kept separately from the Crystal Red / Crystal Black (C. cantonensis 'Crystal' complex) entry per AquaMate's hybrid policy: Taiwan Bee lines are generally regarded as softer-water and more TDS-sensitive than Crystal lines, even though their husbandry windows partially overlap. Reviewers consolidating may wish to merge these later if Stage A discovery surfaces conflicting evidence.

Water parameters are tight and unforgiving — Taiwan Bees are widely considered more delicate than the Crystal complex. Typical hobbyist targets across sources: TDS 80–150 ppm (most sources cluster 100–130 ppm), pH 5.8–6.8 (sources span 5.5–7.5; the tighter range is used here), gH 4–6, kH 0–1, temperature 20–24°C (sources span 18–26°C; the tighter range is used here as the recommended window). Use RO/DI water with a Caridina-specific remineralizer (e.g. Salty Shrimp GH+, Salty Shrimp GH/KH+ is NOT appropriate as it raises kH). An active buffering substrate is generally required to hold pH down.

Highly copper-sensitive — avoid copper-based medications, snail treatments, and tap water with copper plumbing residue. Only add to a fully cycled, mature tank (3+ months recommended) with established biofilm. Stable parameters are widely cited as more important than hitting exact target values.

Cross-source disagreement notes: pH ranges across sources span 5.5–7.5; temperature spans 18–26°C; TDS spans 80–150 ppm. The narrower windows shown above represent the more commonly cited 'sweet spot' across Gensou, Teacher's Pet, and Indoor Ecosystem rather than the union of all source ranges. The Shrimp Farm (originally designated secondary) returned HTTP 403 and was inaccessible; Fishkeeping World and Practical Fishkeeping also 403'd / 404'd. Substituted secondaries are Wikipedia (C. mariae and C. cantonensis), Gensou, Teacher's Pet, and Indoor Ecosystem care guides.
breedingNotes: Generally considered moderately difficult to breed — easier than wild Caridina but much harder than Neocaridina. Females reach sexual maturity at roughly 4–6 months and carry 20–30 eggs underneath the abdomen for approximately 28–35 days. Shrimplets are direct-developing miniatures (no larval stage) and immediately feed on biofilm. Colonies may take up to 6 months to begin reliable production after being added to a new tank. Stable water parameters (TDS within ±10 ppm, no temperature swings), abundant biofilm, sponge filtration to protect shrimplets, and absence of fish predators are widely cited as prerequisites. Mixed-morph colonies will interbreed and typically produce mixed-grade offspring; selective culling is part of the hobby for line preservation.

primary.name: Flip Aquatics
primary.notes: Primary collection page lists Shadow Panda / King Kong, Shadow Mosura, Black King Kong Extreme, Blue Bolt, and Red King Kong / Panda Mix as offered Taiwan Bee morphs. Care parameter details pulled from the linked Flip Aquatics FAQ (https://flipaquatics.com/pages/frequently-asked-questions): temperature 67-72°F (19.4-22.2°C), pH below 7, ~30-day molt frequency, sexual maturity 4-6 months, recommends Salty Shrimp GH+ remineralizer and Brightwell Rio Escuro / Café Soil buffering substrate.

additional.name: The Shrimp Farm
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden on all attempted paths (/posts/taiwan-bee-shrimp-care/, /category/taiwan-bee/, /posts/category/care-guides/). URL retained for reviewer reference; substituted with Wikipedia, Gensou, Teacher's Pet, and Indoor Ecosystem per Stage C fallback policy.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Caridina mariae)
additional.notes: Taxonomy anchor (family Atyidae, order Decapoda, described 2014 by Klotz & von Rintelen, sister to C. cantonensis), native range (Guangdong Province, Hong Kong), adult size 1-1.5 in (2.5-3.8 cm), pH 6-7.5, temperature 18-24°C, lifespan 1-2 years. Also cross-referenced Wikipedia (Caridina cantonensis) for selective breeding history (Crystal Red developed 1996) and 28-day egg incubation.

additional.name: Gensou Premium Aquascaping - Caridina Shrimp Care Guide
additional.notes: Secondary husbandry cross-check. Temperature 22-24°C, pH 5.8-6.5, gH 4-6, kH 0-1, TDS 100-150 ppm, tank 30-60 L minimum, female fecundity 20-30 eggs at 28-35 days. Lists King Kong, Panda, Pinto, Red Ruby/Red Wine, Shadow Bee morph variants. Notes Taiwan Bees are 'slightly more delicate' than Crystal Reds/Blacks despite overlapping parameters.

additional.name: Teacher's Pet - Caridina Water Parameters
additional.notes: Cross-check for water parameters: pH 6.0-6.8, gH 4-6, kH 0-1, TDS 80-150, temperature 18-23°C. Stresses low kH is critical for molting.

additional.name: Indoor Ecosystem - Caridina Shrimp Guide
additional.notes: Cross-check. Temperature 20-24°C, pH 5.5-6.5, gH 4-6, kH 0-1, TDS 90-130 ppm, 10-gallon (~40 L) minimum, explicit copper-sensitivity warning, breeding age 3-5 months, 20-30 eggs at 28-35 days.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally molts roughly every 3-4 weeks, with younger shrimp molting more frequently and adults slowing as they mature. Discarded exoskeletons are left in the tank and re-consumed for calcium — they should not be removed. Molting problems are widely associated with sudden TDS/gH swings, insufficient minerals, or unstable water parameters; the 'white ring of death' incomplete-molt fatality is commonly reported in poorly-buffered or mineral-deficient tanks. Stable, low-kH water with a Caridina-specific remineralizer is widely considered essential.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Breeds in stable, mature tanks with soft acidic water and abundant biofilm; colonies may take up to 6 months to begin reliable production. See top-level breedingNotes for details.
